<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Narita Gion Festival]][[ja:成田祇園祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Narita Gion Festival]][[ja:成田祇園祭]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Narita Gion Festival]][[ja:成田祇園祭]]
'''Narita Gion Festival'''
{{Source Citation|date=2020-05-03}} {{Unified Character|祇}} [[File:Narita-gion-festival-1,Narita-city,Japan.jpg|thumb|right|250px|Festival floats lined up in front of JR{{ill|Narita Station|en|Narita Station|WD=Q845243|lt=Narita Station|1=Narita Station}}]]
'''Narita Gion Festival''' (なりたぎおんさい) is a festival event in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|WD=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}} {{ill|Narita, Chiba|en|Narita, Chiba|WD=Q273798|lt=Narita City|1=Narita, Chiba}} held on the Friday, Saturday, and Sunday closest to the traditional dates (July 7, 8, and 9). It is organized in conjunction with the period of {{ill|Narita-san|en|Narita-san|WD=Q3083875|lt=Naritasan Shinshoji|1=Narita-san}}, a religious event known as "Naritasan Gionkai" (なりたさんぎおんえ)<ref>[http://www.nrtk.jp/news/gion/gione.html What is Naritasan Gionkai? (Narita City Tourism Association Official Site)]</ref>. Naritasan Gionkai is the festival dedicated to {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}}, the principal image of Shinshoji, and to {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|1=Vairocana}}, the local Buddha of the Oku-no-in. The Narita Gion Festival is a large-scale event in which all neighborhoods of Naritasan and Old Narita participate, featuring a {{ill|Mikoshi|ja|御輿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikoshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神輿}} carrying Dainichi Nyorai as its sacred body, and a total of 10 {{ill|Matsuri float|en|Matsuri float|WD=Q11470974|lt=Festival Float|1=Matsuri float}} and stalls paraded simultaneously.
== Origin and History == * Naritasan Gionkai is mentioned in a document from {{ill|1721|en|1721|WD=Q6913|lt=1721|1=1721}} (Kyoho 6) as having held a deity festival on June 8 ({{ill|Old Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}) with the phrase "In Kyoho 6, until the Year of the Ox, the festival dance was performed," indicating that the festival was observed during the Kyoho era. The exact manner in which it was conducted is unknown. Although the origin of the current practice—where festival floats are pulled—is said to date from the late Edo period, the precise details remain unclear. Excluding its specific format, the festival has a history of approximately 300 years. The oldest extant float or stall is that of Kamimachi, produced in the late Edo period by a shrine carpenter from the former Chiba Town; it was originally used in Kisarazu and in the former Nagasu Town of Chiba City, and was later acquired during the Meiji period. Furthermore, among the floats produced specifically for the Narita Gion Festival, the one from Nakanomachi is the oldest (produced in 1900 in Kanda, Tokyo). [[File:Narita-gion-festival2,Narita-city,Japan.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Mikoshi enshrining Dainichi Nyorai of Naritasan Oku-no-in]] * Initially, the festival was centered around the Yudenyama Gongen Shrine, managed by Naritasan (currently located beside the east exit of JR{{ill|Narita Station|en|Narita Station|WD=Q845243|lt=Narita Station|1=Narita Station}}). The {{ill|Ujigami|en|Ujigami|WD=Q767655|lt=Parishioners|1=Ujigami}} extended over 33 villages at the time, and with changing eras—following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period—the festival shifted to one dedicated to Dainichi Nyorai of the Oku-no-in. As the area transformed from a village into a town with the development of roads and shops during the Meiji period, the festival gradually evolved into a form closer to what is observed today. * There are two types of festival music: {{ill|Sawara Hayashi|ja|佐原囃子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sawara Hayashi|1=Sawara Bayashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11383039}} and {{ill|Edo Hayashi|ja|江戸囃子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo Hayashi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江戸川区|1=Edogawa, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q214056}}. In the three neighborhoods possessing Edo-style floats of the hoko-dai type, Edo Hayashi is performed, while in the other seven neighborhoods, Sawara Hayashi is featured.

[Sakura Hayashi]) |}
== Other == * April 18, 2004 (Heisei 16) – In Narita City, Chiba Prefecture, a float festival commemorating the 50th anniversary of the city's establishment was held, and 16 floats and stalls from Narita City, including 10 paraded during the Narita Gion Festival, were pulled in procession. * April 19, 2009 (Heisei 21) – In Narita City, Chiba Prefecture, a float festival commemorating the 55th anniversary of the city's establishment was held, and 15 floats and stalls from Narita City, including 10 paraded during the Narita Gion Festival, were pulled in procession. * April 20, 2014 (Heisei 26) – In Narita City, Chiba Prefecture, a float festival commemorating the 60th anniversary of the city's establishment was held, and 18 floats and stalls from Narita City, including 10 paraded during the Narita Gion Festival, were pulled in procession. * April 20, 2020 (Reiwa 2) – Due to measures to prevent the spread of the novel coronavirus, the Narita Gion Festival scheduled from July 10 to 12 was cancelled. However, the Naritasan Gionkai was held on a reduced scale from July 7 to 12. * April 23, 2021 (Reiwa 3) – Due to measures to prevent the spread of the novel coronavirus, the Narita Gion Festival scheduled from July 9 to 11 was cancelled for the second consecutive year. However, the Naritasan Gionkai was held from July 7 to 11. * Reiwa 4 (2022) – The Narita Gion Festival was held for the first time in three years. In light of infection control measures, the event was scaled down by shortening its duration, limiting the number of participating groups, and omitting the general dance. * Reiwa 5 (2023) – The Narita Gion Festival was held at its usual scale for the first time in four years. * April 28, 2024 (Reiwa 6) – In Narita City, Chiba Prefecture, a float festival commemorating the 70th anniversary of the city's establishment was held, and 18 floats and stalls from Narita City, including 10 paraded during the Narita Gion Festival, were pulled in procession.

== Other == * April 18, 2004 (Heisei 16) – In Narita City, Chiba Prefecture, a float festival commemorating the 50th anniversary of the city's establishment was held, and 16 floats and stalls from Narita City, including 10 paraded during the Narita Gion Festival, were pulled in procession. * April 19, 2009 (Heisei 21) – In Narita City, Chiba Prefecture, a float festival commemorating the 55th anniversary of the city's establishment was held, and 15 floats and stalls from Narita City, including 10 paraded during the Narita Gion Festival, were pulled in procession. * April 20, 2014 (Heisei 26) – In Narita City, Chiba Prefecture, a float festival commemorating the 60th anniversary of the city's establishment was held, and 18 floats and stalls from Narita City, including 10 paraded during the Narita Gion Festival, were pulled in procession. * April 20, 2020 (Reiwa 2) – Due to measures to prevent the spread of the novel coronavirus, the Narita Gion Festival scheduled from July 10 to 12 was cancelled. However, the Naritasan Gionkai was held on a reduced scale from July 7 to 12. * April 23, 2021 (Reiwa 3) – Due to measures to prevent the spread of the novel coronavirus, the Narita Gion Festival scheduled from July 9 to 11 was cancelled for the second consecutive year. However, the Naritasan Gionkai was held from July 7 to 11. * Reiwa 4 (2022) – The Narita Gion Festival was held for the first time in three years. In light of infection control measures, the event was scaled down by shortening its duration, limiting the number of participating groups, and omitting the general dance. * Reiwa 5 (2023) – The Narita Gion Festival was held at its usual scale for the first time in four years. * April 28, 2024 (Reiwa 6) – In Narita City, Chiba Prefecture, a float festival commemorating the 70th anniversary of the city's establishment was held, and 18 floats and stalls from Narita City, including 10 paraded during the Narita Gion Festival, were pulled in procession.
== Access == * Location: Naritasan Shinshoji and the surrounding Omotesando area * By Train: A 10-minute walk from {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Narita Station|en|Narita Station|WD=Q845243|lt=Narita Station|1=Narita Station}} and {{ill|Keisei Narita Station|en|Keisei Narita Station|WD=Q4502163|lt=Keisei Narita Station|1=Keisei Narita Station}}
